Document [7] (Title: Physiology, Bone -- Function -- Bone Extracellular Matrix) The bone extracellular matrix makes up 90% of the overall bone volume and consists of inorganic (mineral) and organic matrices. Inorganic bone matrix accounts for 99% of the body's storage of calcium, 85% of the phosphorus, and 40% to 60% of the magnesium and sodium. It is mainly in the form of hydroxyapatite [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2] to provide the bone its strength, stiffness, and resistance to compressive forces. Organic bone matrix is secreted by osteoblasts and is predominantly type I collagen; this also contains glycoproteins, growth factors, and proteoglycans. Growth factors (such as osteocalcin, osteonectin, and bone sialoprotein) play important roles in osteoid formation, mineralization, and bone remodeling. The organic matrix gives bone its form and provides resistance to tensile forces. [7]

Document [10] (Title: Sea Urchin Toxicity -- Introduction) Sea urchins are part of the phylum Echinodermata which also includes starfish. Sea urchins have globular bodies covered by calcified spines. The spines are either rounded at the tip or hollow for envenomation. The also can have pedicellariae that can grasp and envenomate, typically with more venom than in the spines. Echinoderms possess a distinctive endoskeletal tissue called stereom, which is composed of calcite organized into a mesh-like structure, in addition to dermal cells and fibers. Stereom forms structural elements that can embed into human tissue as spines. When stepped on, these spines cause painful puncture wounds with immediate pain, bleeding, and edema. They can cause severe muscle ache which can last up to 24 hours. [1] [2] [3]

Document [22] (Title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Function) Bone mineralization : phosphate is responsible for mineralization of the bony matrix. This process begins in the matrix vesicle, which are extracellular structures derived from the cell membrane of the osteoblast and chondrocytes. Matrix vesicles acquire phosphate by two pathways: Tissue nonspecific Alkaline phosphatase present within matrix vesicles hydrolyzes phosphoric esters to inorganic phosphate. Matrix vesicles uptake extracellular phosphate via Type II Na/Pi cotransporter. Matrix vesicles form hydroxyapatite crystals from calcium and phosphate; these crystals mineralize the extracellular matrix of the bone.

Document [24] (Title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development) Enamel formation (Amelogenesis): Enamel formation starts immediately after the first layer of dentin is laid down by the odontoblasts. The cells from the inner enamel epithelium differentiate into ameloblasts. These are columnar cells attached to the stratum intermedium via ita base. A pyramidal extension is present at the secretory end of ameloblasts known as Tomes' process through which the enamel matrix is secreted at the amelodentinal junction. Once the enamel matrix is laid down, immediately, the mineralization process begins by secretion of calcium and phosphate ions into the enamel matrix, forming hydroxyapatite crystallites. As more matrixes are secreted and mineralized, the ameloblast cells move away from the amelodentinal junction forming a pattern of crystallites contained within enamel prisms. These prisms are also known as enamel rods as they run from the amelodentinal junction to the enamel surface.

Document [28] (Title: Calcium Carbonate -- Mechanism of Action) Calcium is naturally present in the body. Most of the body's stored calcium is within the bones and teeth as hydroxyapatite. The rest of the body's calcium is in blood, extracellular fluid, muscle, and other body tissues. [19] In the blood, 40% of calcium is bound to albumin, 13% binds to an anion, like phosphate and lactate, and the majority, 47%, is free, unbound, ionized calcium (Ca 2+ ). The amount of ionized calcium found in the blood is essential and tightly regulated by homeostatic controls because it is physiologically active and plays a vital role in the cellular processes of excitable tissues, such as blood vessels, muscles, glands, and nerves. [1] Calcium carbonate has three separate mechanisms of action that have pharmacologic effects. Calcium affects the stomach, small intestine, and blood. [20]
Document [29] (Title: Physiology, Bone Remodeling -- Introduction) Bones are not inert structures within the human body; they continue to change throughout the lifespan. Remodeling entails the resorption of old or damaged bone, followed by the deposition of new bone material.
Document [30] (Title: Methods of Estimation of Time Since Death -- Function -- Extreme Decomposition Phase) The phase of extreme decomposition is seen only in remains that have been exposed to the environment and lead to erosion of the skeletal elements. This erosion begins with the process of bleaching of bones and is commonly seen six months after exposure, although it has been documented to appear as early as two months after death and as late as two and a half years post-mortem. The skeletal elements undergo further degeneration of the cortical structure, resulting in a metaphyseal loss in long bones and exposure of the cancellous part of spongy bones; this is seen commonly between a year to a year and a half after death, although it has been reported to have occurred as early as the fourth month. The metaphyseal loss was reported to have occurred at a PMI of five and a half years. [18]
Document [31] (Title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Introduction) Nutrients are chemical substances required by the body to sustain basic functions and are optimally obtained by eating a balanced diet. There are six major classes of nutrients essential for human health: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, vitamins, minerals, and water. Carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins are considered macronutrients and serve as a source of energy. Water is required in large amounts but does not yield energy. Vitamins and minerals are considered micronutrients and play essential roles in metabolism. Vitamins are organic micronutrients classified as either water-soluble or fat-soluble. The essential water-soluble vitamins include vitamins B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12, and C. The essential fat-soluble vitamins include vitamins A, E, D, and K. Minerals are inorganic micronutrients. Minerals can classify as macrominerals or microminerals. Macrominerals are required in amounts greater than 100 mg per day and include calcium, phosphorous, magnesium, sodium, potassium, and chloride. Sodium, potassium, and chloride are also electrolytes. Microminerals are those nutrients required in amounts less than 100 mg per day and include iron, copper, zinc, selenium, and iodine. This article will review the following biochemical aspects of the essential nutrients: fundamentals, cellular, molecular, function, testing, and clinical significance. [1] [2]

Functionally, vitamin D exerts an inhibitory effect on bone resorption (inhibitory kostní resorpce) and is directly related to osteoporosis (هشاشة العظام), underscoring its critical role in maintaining bone health and preventing bone loss. Together, these connections highlight vitamin D’s central position as a precursor to the steroid hormone kalcitriol, a component of various supplements, and a key regulator of skeletal metabolism.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2891) Mineral supplements represent a category of nutritional products that provide essential minerals for health maintenance, and they include both calcium protein preparations and bor (boron). Calcium protein preparations are formulations that combine calcium with protein to support bone strength and muscle function, while bor is a trace mineral that contributes to bone metabolism and hormone regulation. Both calcium protein preparations and bor are considered types of mineral supplements used to address mineral deficiencies and promote musculoskeletal health in patient care.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1984) Selenium supplement is classified as an “other mineral supplement” within the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) system, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of mineral supplements.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2328) Mineral supplements are classified as calcium protein preparations and are formulations that contain the elements bor (boron) and molybden (molybdenum). In the hierarchical taxonomy of chemical substances, the category Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds lists both bor and molybden as child elements, indicating that these trace elements belong to this broader group. Consequently, mineral supplements, through their inclusion of bor and molybden, are linked to the Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds classification, highlighting their role as nutrient sources of specific trace minerals within the larger context of elemental chemistry. This integrated view aids in understanding the composition of mineral supplements and their placement within chemical taxonomy for clinical and nutritional considerations.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449) Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179) Kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are both classified as members of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, indicating that they are vitamin D–related compounds that share similar biological activity in regulating calcium and phosphate metabolism. This classification highlights their common therapeutic role as vitamin D analogues used to treat conditions such as hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and certain bone disorders.

In multivitamin formulations vitamin D works synergistically with calcium, iron, vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin E, the full B‑vitamin complex, and trace minerals to provide complementary nutritional benefits: calcium pantothenate supports cellular metabolism within the B‑vitamin spectrum, while vitamin D promotes skeletal health. Collectively, these nutrients—vitamin A (various chemical forms), niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) and vitamin D—are extensively utilized as active or inactive ingredients across oral multivitamin‑mineral products, prenatal supplements, DHA‑enriched blends, iron‑fortified formulations and topical dermatologic preparations. Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825) Ascorbic acid and calcium together form a specific formulation that is classified as a vitamin C combination, indicating that this pairing is recognized as one of the various combinations that incorporate vitamin C (ascorbic acid) with calcium to potentially enhance nutritional or therapeutic effects.
